reference_answer: Warning Letter 320-26-22; MARCS-CMS 717355; issued December 1, 2025.
Recipient: Venkata R. Gogineni, Ph.D., CEO of Seaway Pharma Inc. The salutation reads "Dear Dr. Gogineni and Mr. Lingam," so it is addressed both to Dr. Gogineni and to Mr. Lingam.
Seaway Pharma Inc., 5 County Route 42, Massena, NY 13662, United States.
Issuing Office: Office of Manufacturing Quality. Signed by Francis Godwin, Director, Office of Manufacturing Quality, Office of Compliance, Center for Drug Evaluation and Research.
Kingston Pharma, LLC; previous warning letter dated May 14, 2019 (CMS #572904).
Over-the-counter (OTC) oral liquid drug products including, but not limited to, analgesics and cough and cold products marketed for infants and children.
From May 19 to June 18, 2025.
September 2021, September 2023, and June 2025.
Within 15 working days after receiving the letter.

(1) Previous Kingston Pharma warning letter (May 14, 2019); (2) first subsequent inspection (September 2021); (3) first regulatory meeting (May 3, 2022); (4) second subsequent inspection (September 2023); (5) second regulatory meeting (May 13, 2024); (6) current inspection (May 19 to June 18, 2025); (7) Seaway Pharma's Form FDA 483 response (July 30, 2025); (8) this warning letter (December 1, 2025).

1. Failed to establish adequate written responsibilities and procedures applicable to the quality control unit and to follow such written procedures (21 CFR 211.22(d)). 2. Failed to conduct at least one test to verify the identity of each component of a drug product (21 CFR 211.84(d)(1)). 3. Failed to establish adequate written procedures for production and process control designed to assure that the drug products have the identity, strength, quality, and purity they purport or are represented to possess (21 CFR 211.100(a)).
Section 501(a)(2)(B) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act), 21 U.S.C. 351(a)(2)(B).
Section 501(b) of the FD&C Act, 21 U.S.C. 351(b).
Glycerin and sorbitol solution (with sorbitol 70% specifically cited).
Between April 2024 and May 2025.

21 CFR 211.165(a) — failure to ensure that each batch of drug products was tested for the strength of active ingredients prior to release; and 21 CFR 211.194(a) — failure to ensure that laboratory records included complete data derived from all tests necessary to ensure compliance with established specifications and standards.

The violations cited in the letter are not intended to be an all-inclusive list of violations that exist at the facility; the firm is responsible for investigating and determining the causes of any violations and for preventing their recurrence or the occurrence of other violations.

Seaway Pharma stated: "Currently, there are no enforceable provisions in either the United States Code or the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) regarding: 1) Defined acceptance criteria for DEG and/or EG." FDA responded that identity testing is required under 21 CFR 211.84(d)(1) and that the limits for DEG and EG tests are specified in the United States Pharmacopeia (USP), which is enforceable under section 501(b) of the FD&C Act, 21 U.S.C. 351(b).

The letter concludes that "executive management oversight and control over the manufacture of drugs are inadequate." Although there was a subsequent transfer of ownership of the company, the manufacturing facility and multiple responsible officials remained the same, and both current and past owners had been notified of the violations through the 2019 warning letter, the September 2021 and September 2023 inspections, and the May 3, 2022 and May 13, 2024 regulatory meetings, but the firm continued to fail to implement quality commitments within a timely manner.
Send the electronic reply to CDER-OC-OMQ-Communications@fda.hhs.gov, identifying the response with FEI 3012275336 and ATTN: Lynnsey Renn.

The letter combines: (1) a prior May 14, 2019 warning letter to predecessor Kingston Pharma, LLC (CMS #572904) citing similar CGMP violations at the same facility with multiple of the same responsible officials; (2) repeated failures shown by subsequent inspections in September 2021, September 2023, and June 2025; (3) persistent violations communicated to the firm at two regulatory meetings on May 3, 2022 and May 13, 2024; and (4) the most recent May 19 - June 18, 2025 inspection that produced three numbered CGMP violations (21 CFR 211.22(d), 211.84(d)(1), 211.100(a)) plus the inadequacy of the firm's July 30, 2025 Form FDA 483 response — together demonstrating that, while current and past owners have been notified, the firm continues to fail to implement quality commitments within a timely manner.
